name: European History Pub Quiz 🍺
description: 40 questions across Ukraine, the Baltics, Poland, Germany, France, and the UK. Fun distractors, pub quiz energy.

questions.text: What is the capital of Ukraine?
questions.explanation: Kyiv (also spelled Kyiv) is Ukraine's capital and largest city, founded in the 5th century. It predates Moscow by several centuries.

options.text: Lviv — the cultural capital, but not THE capital
Kyiv
Kharkiv — close, but no cigar
Odessa — lovely port city, wrong answer though

questions.text: In which year did the Chernobyl nuclear disaster occur?
questions.explanation: Reactor No. 4 at the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant exploded on 26 April 1986 — the worst nuclear accident in history. The nearby city of Pripyat was evacuated within 36 hours.

options.text: 1982 — when ABBA broke up (also a disaster)
1984 — George Orwell's favourite year
1989 — walls were falling, but not this one

questions.text: Ukraine is the largest country located entirely within Europe — true or false?
questions.explanation: True! Ukraine covers 603,550 km², making it the largest country lying entirely within Europe. Russia is bigger overall but spans two continents.

options.text: Yes
No

questions.text: In which year did Ukraine declare independence from the Soviet Union?
questions.explanation: Ukraine declared independence on 24 August 1991, confirmed by a referendum on 1 December 1991 with over 90% in favour. The USSR formally dissolved on 25 December 1991. Image: Kyiv — Wikipedia Commons, CC BY-SA.

options.text: 1989 — wall-falling season, but Ukraine waited
1990 — close but still Soviet
1993 — fashionably late

questions.text: The national flower of Ukraine is the sunflower — true or false?
questions.explanation: True! The sunflower (соняшник) is Ukraine's national flower. Ukraine is one of the world's top sunflower oil producers — so the symbol is very apt.

options.text: Yes
No

questions.text: Kyiv was founded centuries before Moscow — true or false?
questions.explanation: True! Kyiv was established around the 5th century AD; Moscow only appears in chronicles in 1147. That's a ~600 year head start. Image: Chernobyl reactor today — Wikipedia Commons, CC BY-SA.

options.text: Yes
No

questions.text: Which three countries make up the Baltic States? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The Baltic States are Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania — three countries on the eastern shore of the Baltic Sea that regained independence from the USSR in 1991. Belarus is NOT a Baltic State.

options.text: Estonia
Latvia
Lithuania
Belarus — close geographically, wrong answer

questions.text: What is the capital of Estonia?
questions.explanation: Tallinn! Its medieval Old Town is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the best-preserved in Northern Europe. Riga is Latvia's capital, Vilnius is Lithuania's. Image: Tallinn Old Town — Wikipedia Commons, CC BY-SA.

options.text: Riga — that's Latvia, nice try
Helsinki — that's Finland, completely different country
Tallinn
Vilnius — that's Lithuania, three for three wrong

questions.text: What is the capital of Latvia?
questions.explanation: Riga is Latvia's capital and largest city. It's also the largest city in all three Baltic States, known for its Art Nouveau architecture — the highest concentration in Europe.

options.text: Vilnius — still Lithuania
Tallinn — still Estonia
Kaunas — second city of Lithuania, not even close
Riga

questions.text: All three Baltic States joined NATO in the same year. Which year?
questions.explanation: Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania all joined NATO on 29 March 2004 — the biggest single enlargement in NATO history, along with 4 other countries. They also joined the EU just weeks later in May 2004.

options.text: 1999 — too early, they were still negotiating
2002 — warmer, still wrong
2007 — Bulgaria and Romania joined then, not Baltics

questions.text: Which Baltic State was the FIRST Soviet republic ever to declare independence, in March 1990?
questions.explanation: Lithuania declared independence from the USSR on 11 March 1990 — the first Soviet republic to do so, sparking a cascade of independence declarations across the bloc. Image: Vilnius — Wikipedia Commons, CC BY-SA.

options.text: Estonia — independent-minded, but third in line
Latvia — plucky, but second
Lithuania
Ukraine — brave, but not until August 1991

questions.text: What is the capital of Lithuania?
questions.explanation: Vilnius. Note: Kaunas is Lithuania's second city and was the provisional capital during the interwar period (1920–1940) when Vilnius was occupied by Poland.

options.text: Kaunas — was temporary capital, doesn't count
Tallinn — still Estonia, are you even trying?
Riga — still Latvia
Vilnius

questions.text: Lithuania is famous for its near-religious passion for which Olympic team sport?
questions.explanation: Basketball is practically a national religion in Lithuania. In the 1992 Barcelona Olympics, the Grateful Dead helped fund Lithuania's team (the skeleton logo on their jerseys!). Lithuania has won 3 EuroBasket titles.

options.text: Football — they like it, not obsessed
Ice Hockey — that's more of a Latvian/Finnish thing
Basketball
Handball — popular in Germany, not Lithuania

questions.text: At its 15th-century peak, which of these modern capitals were inside the Grand Duchy of Lithuania? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: At its 15th-century peak, the Grand Duchy of Lithuania stretched from the Baltic to the Black Sea, encompassing modern Vilnius, Minsk, and Kyiv. Warsaw was in the Kingdom of Poland (their ally-rival next door).

options.text: Vilnius (Lithuania) — obviously
Minsk (Belarus) — yes, medieval Belarus was very Lithuanian
Kyiv (Ukraine) — also yes, surprisingly
Warsaw (Poland) — nope, different kingdom

questions.text: What is the capital of Poland?
questions.explanation: Warsaw (Warszawa) is the capital. Fun fact: it was almost completely destroyed in WWII and was entirely rebuilt from scratch — including from 18th-century Canaletto paintings. A true phoenix city.

options.text: Kraków — was the capital until 1596, then got demoted
Gdańsk — lovely port city, Solidarity vibes
Warsaw
Wrocław — was German Breslau until 1945, plot twist

questions.text: In which year did Germany invade Poland, starting World War II?
questions.explanation: Germany invaded Poland on 1 September 1939, triggering declarations of war from Britain and France two days later. The USSR invaded from the east on 17 September under the secret Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact.

options.text: 1937 — the Nazis were busy in Spain then
1938 — that was Czechoslovakia's bad year
1940 — by then France was already having a terrible time

questions.text: Marie Curie, first woman to win a Nobel Prize, was born in which city?
questions.explanation: Marie Skłodowska-Curie was born in Warsaw in 1867. She won not one but TWO Nobel Prizes — Physics (1903) and Chemistry (1911). Still the only person to win in two different sciences.

options.text: Paris — where she became famous, not where she was born
Vienna — stylish guess, completely wrong
Prague — almost Eastern Europe, wrong country
Warsaw

questions.text: The Polish trade union that led the collapse of communist rule in Poland was called what?
questions.explanation: Solidarity (Solidarność) was founded in 1980 at the Gdańsk shipyard under Lech Wałęsa. It grew to 10 million members and became the first independent trade union in the Soviet bloc. Wałęsa won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1983.

options.text: Workers' Unity — sounds right, completely made up
Solidarity
Liberation Front — too French Resistance
Free Poland — too on-the-nose, also fake

questions.text: Poland joined the European Union in which year?
questions.explanation: Poland joined the EU on 1 May 2004 as part of the largest single enlargement, alongside 9 other countries. Poland is now the EU's 5th largest economy and largest member from Central/Eastern Europe.

options.text: 2000 — millennium enthusiasm, but no
2002 — when the Euro coins launched, but not Poland's EU entry
2007 — that's Bulgaria and Romania

questions.text: Pierogi are a beloved Polish dish. What exactly are they?
questions.explanation: Pierogi are filled dumplings — boiled (then often pan-fried) with fillings like potato & cheese, sauerkraut & mushroom, or sweet fillings like strawberry. The Polish answer to ravioli, and infinitely more comforting.

options.text: Grilled sausages — that's kielbasa, different delicious thing
Stuffed dumplings
Potato pancakes — that's placki ziemniaczane, also excellent
Pickled herring in cream sauce — that's Scandinavia's problem

questions.text: What is the capital of Germany?
questions.explanation: Berlin! It was divided into East and West from 1945–1990 and only became the (re-unified) capital again in 1990. Bonn served as West Germany's capital during the Cold War. Image: The Berlin Wall — Wikipedia Commons, CC BY-SA.

options.text: Munich — capital of Bavaria and beer halls
Hamburg — capital of excellent fish sandwiches
Frankfurt — capital of EU finance and airport delays
Berlin

questions.text: In which year did the Berlin Wall fall?
questions.explanation: The Wall fell on 9 November 1989 after an East German spokesman accidentally announced — live on TV — that border crossings were open 'immediately, without delay'. Crowds stormed the checkpoints that night.

options.text: 1987 — when Reagan said 'tear down this wall' (it wasn't torn down then)
1988 — close, but Berliners waited one more year
1991 — by then even the USSR was gone

questions.text: Germany was officially reunified on 3 October of which year?
questions.explanation: German reunification happened on 3 October 1990 — now celebrated as German Unity Day (Tag der Deutschen Einheit). The process from Wall fall to reunification took less than 11 months.

options.text: 1989 — the Wall fell but unification took more paperwork
1991 — the year the USSR dissolved, coincidence?
1992 — by then they were busy with the Maastricht Treaty

questions.text: How many federal states (Bundesländer) does Germany have?
questions.explanation: Germany has 16 Bundesländer (federal states). 10 were in West Germany, 5 in East Germany, plus Berlin (which was split). They reunited as 16 on 3 October 1990.

options.text: 12 — that's months in a year
14 — that's a common wrong guess
18 — too many bratwurst regions

questions.text: Oktoberfest is held in which German city?
questions.explanation: Munich (München), Bavaria! Oktoberfest began in 1810 for Crown Prince Ludwig's wedding and has been going ever since. Despite the name, it mostly takes place in September. Around 6 million litres of beer are consumed annually. Image: Neuschwanstein Castle, Bavaria — Wikipedia Commons, CC BY-SA.

options.text: Berlin — they have their own wild parties, different vibe
Frankfurt — home of the airport and the ECB, not lederhosen
Hamburg — famous for fish and a certain band's early gigs
Munich

questions.text: Which of these famous composers were born in what is now Germany? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Bach (Eisenach), Beethoven (Bonn), and Handel (Halle) were all born in German lands. Chopin was born near Warsaw, Poland — and spent most of his adult life in Paris feeling homesick.

options.text: Johann Sebastian Bach
Ludwig van Beethoven
George Frideric Handel
Frédéric Chopin — Polish! (and very sad about it)

questions.text: In which year did the French Revolution begin?
questions.explanation: The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of the Bastille on 14 July — now celebrated as Bastille Day. It ended (debatably) in 1799 when Napoleon seized power. Image: Eiffel Tower — Wikipedia Commons, CC BY-SA.

options.text: 1776 — that was America's revolution, totally different accent
1799 — that's when Napoleon ended it and took over
1804 — Napoleon was already Emperor by then

questions.text: What is the French national motto?
questions.explanation: Liberté, Égalité, Fraternité — Freedom, Equality, Brotherhood. It became the official motto of the French Republic after the Revolution. It appears on coins, government buildings, and official documents.

options.text: Vive la France — a cheer, not a motto
Honneur et Patrie — that's the French Navy
Paix, Justice, Liberté — invented this second
Liberté, Égalité, Fraternité

questions.text: In which year was the Eiffel Tower completed?
questions.explanation: The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1889 for the World's Fair (Exposition Universelle) marking the centennial of the French Revolution. Gustave Eiffel's team built it in just 2 years, 2 months, and 5 days.

options.text: 1878 — Paris had a World's Fair then too, no tower though
1885 — that's when the Statue of Liberty arrived in New York
1900 — next World's Fair, tower was already old news

questions.text: Napoleon Bonaparte was defeated at the Battle of Waterloo in which year?
questions.explanation: Napoleon was defeated at Waterloo on 18 June 1815 by the Duke of Wellington (British) and Gebhard Leberecht von Blücher (Prussian). He was then exiled to Saint Helena, where he died in 1821. Image: Napoleon — Jacques-Louis David, Wikipedia Commons, Public Domain.

options.text: 1812 — that was his Moscow disaster, different humiliation
1813 — Leipzig (Battle of Nations), still not Waterloo
1814 — first abdication, first exile to Elba — came back

questions.text: France is the world's most visited country by international tourists — true or false?
questions.explanation: True! France has topped the global tourism rankings for decades with ~100 million international visitors annually. Paris alone receives more tourists than any other city.

options.text: Yes
No

questions.text: The Louvre in Paris is the world's most visited art museum — true or false?
questions.explanation: True! The Louvre attracts around 9 million visitors per year. The Mona Lisa is so swamped by crowds that you need binoculars to see the actual painting. It's 77cm × 53cm. Smaller than you expected.

options.text: Yes
No

questions.text: In which year did the Norman Conquest of England take place (Battle of Hastings)?
questions.explanation: 1066 — the most famous date in English history. William the Conqueror defeated King Harold at the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066 and was crowned King of England on Christmas Day. He then built the Tower of London. Image: Tower of London — Wikipedia Commons, CC BY-SA.

options.text: 1042 — Edward the Confessor's coronation, no conquest
1086 — that's the Domesday Book, William's tax census
1100 — William II's death, accidentally shot hunting

questions.text: The Magna Carta, signed in 1215, primarily did what?
questions.explanation: Magna Carta (Great Charter) was forced on King John by rebellious barons in June 1215. It established that even the king was subject to the rule of law. Just 3 of its 63 clauses are still in force in English law today.

options.text: Established the Church of England — that was Henry VIII, 300 years later
Declared war on France — practically England's default setting anyway
Created the first English Parliament — that came a bit later
Limited the power of the English king

questions.text: In which year did women first gain the right to vote in the UK (women over 30)?
questions.explanation: The Representation of the People Act 1918 gave the vote to women over 30 (with property qualifications). Full equal suffrage came in 1928. The suffragette movement, led by the Pankhursts, had campaigned for decades.

options.text: 1912 — close, the Titanic year, still no votes for women
1928 — that's when ALL women over 21 got the vote
1933 — by then women were voting in many European countries

questions.text: At its peak, the British Empire covered roughly what share of Earth's land surface?
questions.explanation: At its peak around 1920, the British Empire covered ~24% of Earth's land area — 35.5 million km². It included about 412 million people (23% of the world's population at the time).

options.text: About 10% — modest guess
About 15% — reasonable but low
About 20% — getting warmer
About 25% — 'the sun never sets' wasn't just marketing

questions.text: William Shakespeare was born and raised in which English town?
questions.explanation: Shakespeare was born in Stratford-upon-Avon, Warwickshire, in April 1564. He moved to London as an adult to pursue theatre, but retired back to Stratford in his later years. Image: William Shakespeare (Chandos portrait) — Wikipedia Commons, Public Domain.

options.text: London — where he worked, not where he was born
Oxford — where clever people studied, not where he lived
Stratford-upon-Avon
Canterbury — Chaucer's territory, different literary genius

questions.text: In which year did the UK officially leave the European Union?
questions.explanation: Brexit was completed on 31 January 2020 (EU departure) with the transition period ending 31 December 2020. The referendum was in June 2016 (52% Leave), but the actual paperwork took 3.5 chaotic years.

options.text: 2016 — the referendum, not the exit
2018 — they were still arguing about the deal
2019 — three missed deadlines later, still no

questions.text: Which of the following are real British monarchs? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Henry VIII (6 wives), George III (lost America), and Edward the Confessor (built Westminster Abbey) were all real monarchs. King Arthur is a legend — no historical evidence he existed as a king.

options.text: Henry VIII — very real, very married
George III — real, lost the American colonies at a card game (sort of)
Edward the Confessor — real, holy, built Westminster Abbey
King Arthur — legendary, fictional, great story though

questions.text: What is the smallest country in Europe by area?
questions.explanation: Vatican City covers just 0.44 km² — smaller than most city parks. Monaco (2 km²), San Marino (61 km²), and Liechtenstein (160 km²) are all larger. Vatican City is also the smallest country in the world.

options.text: Monaco — glamorous but 5× larger than Vatican
Liechtenstein — sounds small, actually 160 km²
San Marino — ancient republic but 140× bigger than Vatican
Vatican City — 0.44 km², smaller than most golf courses

questions.text: The Eurovision Song Contest was first held in which year?
questions.explanation: Eurovision began in 1956 in Lugano, Switzerland — only 7 countries participated and Switzerland won. It's now one of the world's most-watched non-sporting events with ~160 million viewers. The winner: 'Refrain' by Lys Assia.

options.text: 1948 — the year the UN declared human rights, no Eurovision yet
1952 — Helsinki Olympics year, not Eurovision
1964 — by then it was already very glittery

questions.text: On a scale of 1–10, how well do you think you know European history?
questions.explanation: No wrong answers — just a vibe check! Now you know a bit more than when you started.
